texts.label: text
texts.orig: Biomass gasification is a viable solution for generating H2; however, the syngas produced must 2 be upgraded to make H2-containing streams suitable for other applications, such as renewable 3 methanol production, by adjusting the H2/CO ratio. 4 In this study, for the first time in literature, the separation properties of carbon hollow fibre 5 membranes in binary and quaternary H2-containing mixtures with varying compositions were 6 systematically explored. It was found that the developed carbon membranes exhibit relatively 7 good  selectivity  for  (H2+CO2)  in  mixtures  with  other  gases  such  as  N2  and  CO,  which  are 8 commonly present in syngas. 9 A CO permeance of 0.8-1 GPU and an H2/CO selectivity of 30 were achieved for the first time 10 through both single gas and mixed gas permeation testing. Furthermore, in the context of using 11 these membranes for syngas upgrading, such as in an integrated biomass-to-biofuel (methanol) 12 process  for  hydrogen  enrichment  or  carbon  capture  and  conversion,  a  technical  feasibility 13 analysis based on the separation performance of the carbon membrane system for syngas ratio 14 adjustment and N2 removal was carried out. The results indicate that the prepared carbon 15 membranes have the potential to adjust H2/CO ratio to 1-3 if a N2 removal ranging from 80 to 16 90 % is acceptable. 17

texts.orig: Global energy demand is expected to increase exponentially in coming years with a more and 25 more accretive increase. Following the direction for minimizing the use of fossil fuels, Countries 26 have turned their attention to the use of renewable energy sources in addition to fossil fuels to 27 meet their increasing energy needs and to obtain clean energy. 28 Hydrogen is a beacon of hope in the quest to curb CO2 emissions and mitigate the greenhouse 29 effect [1, 2]. As a non-carbon fuel, its combustion yields nothing but water. Currently, global 30 hydrogen demand is primarily driven by ammonia synthesis (51 %), oil refining (31 %), and 31 methanol  synthesis  (10  %)  [ 3 ].  To  meet  this  demand,  approximately  95  million  tons  of 32 hydrogen are produced annually [4]. The European Commission has set an ambitious target to 33 produce and import 10 million tons of renewable hydrogen by 2030 through water electrolysis, 34 underpinned  by  the  hydrogen  accelerator  concept  [5].  However,  hydrogen  does  not  occur 35 naturally in its pure form. It must be extracted from fossil fuels (via steam reforming [6], partial 36 oxidation,  or  coal  gasification)  or  renewable  sources  (via  biomass  gasification  [7]  or  water 37 splitting  using  solar/wind  energy  [8, 9,10, 11, 12].  Hydrogen  production  technologies  are 38 classified into three categories based on their CO2 emissions: positive, free, and neutral [13]. 39 The most appealing category is neutral CO2 emissions, which can be achieved using biomass as 40 a feedstock through biological (dark and photo fermentation, biophotolysis) or thermochemical 41 processes (gasification and pyrolysis) [14]. 42 Although  gasification  is  a  technique  that  produces  the  largest  amount  of  CO2,  and  has  the 43 highest carbon footprint [15] it is still among the most used hydrogen production processes 44 [16, 17, 18]. The main reason for this is that most existing industrial plants are large scale which 45 makes switchover  to  new  technologies  slower.  In  addition,  unexpected  events,  such  as  the 46 recent global energy crisis, are directing governments to activate solutions for the production 47 of energy based on fossil fuels [19]. Alternatively, transforming raw materials and by-products, 48 such as coal, wood, plastic waste [20] and solid waste [21], sawdust, car tires and sewage sludge 49

Through the gasification process carbon-containing materials are converted into synthesis gas (see Figure S1), which is a gas mixture containing some or all of H2, CH4, CO, NH3, H2S, N2, HCl and HCN and also ash and tar [23]. The typical composition of syngas mixture ranges CO 18-20 %, H2 15-20 %, CH4 1-5 %, CO2 9-12 % and N2 45-55 %, without considering contaminants and other substance, even though it differs depending on fuel type and gasification method. Values for the downdraft method and eight different fuel types were reported by Beohar et al. [ 24 ](Table S2).

texts.orig: In order to comply with the directives concerning the reduction of greenhouse gas emissions, development of new, but also improvement of existing, technologies must be given high priority by governments and the scientific community. To this end, research was intensified over the last decade on modes of operating coal-fired power plants with carbon capture and storage. In particular,  pre-combustion  options  via  coal  gasification,  especially  integrated  gasification combined cycle processes, are attracting the attention of governments, industry and research community as a fast-adaptation alternative to conventional power generation [25]. Moreover, the biomass gasification process can also produce syngas, but the challenges and demands of syngas cleaning must be addressed [26]. For example, based on the fact that wood gasification produces a gas mixture with a high concentration of N2 (Table S2), removing most of N2, can lead to a final enriched mixture that could be used for methanol production, given that global methanol capacity is poised to see considerable growth over the next five years, potentially increasing  from  171.8  MTPA  (Million  Tonnes  per  Annum)  in  2022  to  302  MTPA  in  2027, registering total growth of 75.8 % [27]. Industrial production of methanol is currently being carried out by reforming fossil-derived syngas (primary CO and H2) over metal-based catalysts at 50-100 bar and 200-300 °C. texts.orig: demands substantial energy input [25]. If flare gas is used for methanol production, the use of a separation technology able to simultaneously separate CH4, CO and N2 from CO2 and H2 could significantly enhance the efficiency of the process, as suggested by Khanipour et al. [28] who proposed the use of selective membranes towards CO2+H2 (step-1 of Figure S2) to adjust the H2/CO  ratio,  while  keeping  a  high  hydrogen  recovery  after  the  enrichment.  Membrane technology  is  emerging  as  a  formidable  challenger  to  conventional  hydrogen  separation techniques  such  as  pressure  swing  adsorption  and  cryogenic  distillation.  It  boasts  several advantages, including lower costs, reduced energy consumption, simplicity, and compactness, making it a more economically and environmentally viable option. However, commercial polymeric membranes usually present relatively low selectivity for H2 purification, while inorganic membranes such as the palladium-based or ceramic membranes are highly cost, which limits their large-scale applications in this field. The emerging carbon membranes showed the promising performances for H2 separation and purification based on the  molecular  sieving  mechanism  to  efficiently  separate  hydrogen  from  other  larger  gas molecules (e.g., CO2, N2, CH4, etc.) [29,30]. In  our  previous  work  [ 31 ]  we  have  demonstrated  the  capability  of  hollow  fibre  carbon membranes to operate stably also in presence of contaminants and water vapour for more than 180 days of continuous testing. In this work, carbon hollow fibre membranes were developed to selectively separate H2 and CO2. These membranes were used to separate H2 from binary (H2/CO2,  H2/CO,  H2/CH4)  and  quaternary  (CO2,  N2,  H2,  CO)  mixtures  of  different  feed concentrations and temperatures. The mutual influence of gases in H2-containing mixtures on membrane permeation and the corresponding variations in separation properties compared to single  gas  measurements  were  systematically  investigated.  This  led  to  a  comprehensive understanding  of  the  differences  in  permeance  and  selectivity  observed  in  mixed-gas conditions. texts.orig: Microcrystalline cellulose (MCC, Avicel PH-101) was used as a polymer precursor for carbon hollow fibre membranes. Specifically, the MCC was dissolved into a co-solvent of 1-Ethyl-3methylimidazolium acetate (EmimAc, >95 %, purchased from IOLITEC GmbH) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, purchased from Sigma-Aldrich) at 60 °C. The weight ratios of MCC, EmimAc, and  DMSO  were  controlled  at  12,  66  and  22  %wt,  respectively . The  cellulose  hollow  fibre membrane precursors were then fabricated by a dry-wet spinning during which a bore solution consisted of 80 %wt co-solvent and 20 %wt ID water. Before being dried in a lab atmosphere with a temperature of ca. 25 °C relative humidity of ca. 30 %, the as-spun fresh cellulose hollow fibres were soaked in water for 72 h to remove residual co-solvent. Afterward, the precursors were  carbonized  with  an  argon  purge  gas  using  the  carbonization  procedure  described elsewhere  [31, 32].  The  asymmetric  carbon  membranes  with  an  average  thickness  for  the selective layer of 3 µm were successfully prepared and reported in the previous work [32 , 33]. A  hollow  fibre  membrane  module  containing  five  carbon  hollow  fibre  membranes  with  an effective membrane area of 9.5 cm 2  was then constructed in a 3/8-inch stainless steel tube sealed by epoxy adhesive, which was used for gas permeation testing.

Specifically, the MCC was dissolved into a co-solvent of 1-Ethyl-3methylimidazolium acetate (EmimAc, >95 %, purchased from IOLITEC GmbH) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, purchased from Sigma-Aldrich) at 60 °C. The weight ratios of MCC, EmimAc, and  DMSO  were  controlled  at  12,  66  and  22  %wt,  respectively . The  cellulose  hollow  fibre membrane precursors were then fabricated by a dry-wet spinning during which a bore solution consisted of 80 %wt co-solvent and 20 %wt ID water. Before being dried in a lab atmosphere with a temperature of ca. 25 °C relative humidity of ca. 30 %, the as-spun fresh cellulose hollow fibres were soaked in water for 72 h to remove residual co-solvent. Afterward, the precursors were  carbonized  with  an  argon  purge  gas  using  the  carbonization  procedure  described elsewhere  [31, 32].  The  asymmetric  carbon  membranes  with  an  average  thickness  for  the selective layer of 3 µm were successfully prepared and reported in the previous work [32 , 33]. A  hollow  fibre  membrane  module  containing  five  carbon  hollow  fibre  membranes  with  an effective membrane area of 9.5 cm 2  was then constructed in a 3/8-inch stainless steel tube sealed by epoxy adhesive, which was used for gas permeation testing.

texts.orig: The separation properties of the carbon-based membranes were explored in the temperature range 50-80°C for H2, N2, CO2, CH4, CO as single gas and in mixtures ( Table 1 )  at  a  feed  pressure  of  10  bar.  The  experimental  set-up  used  for  the  experiments  is shown in Figure 1. The hollow fibre membrane module was placed in a furnace for precise temperature control. Mass flow controllers (Brooks Instruments 5850S) were used to regulate the flow rate of gases fed to the module. Gas mixtures were either already available in certified bottles or modulated using mass flow controllers. A back pressure controller (Swagelok KBP series) regulated feed pressure and pressure drops were monitored using gauges on the feed and retentate lines. Temperature was finely monitored using sensors on the feed, retentate, and permeate lines (Digitron HLX31PFTE). During experiments, a constant sweep gas flow rate of 5 mL(STP) min 1  was applied and permeate pressure was atmospheric for single gas measurements or slightly higher for mixtures owing to pressure drop from the micro-gas-chromatograph. Separation measurements were carried out under steady state conditions and the membrane module was exposed to a nitrogen stream at a pressure of 10 bar during stand-by periods or changes  in  operating  conditions.  Before  changing  gas  supply,  the  module  was  'washed'  by feeding the new gas/mixture for 30 minutes on both sides of the membrane. The composition of permeate, retentate, and feed was analysed using a micro gas chromatograph (Agilent 990) equipped with different columns (MolSieve 5Å, PoraPLOT Q, CP-Sil 19 CB) and three TCDs. Almost all the results reported in this manuscript showed a standard deviation below 4 %. The figures show error bars only when the error exceeded this value.

texts.orig: Concerning  the  small-scale  scenario  of  syngas  production  from  the  gasification  process,  a single-stage carbon membrane system with a syngas processing capacity of ≤1000 Nm 3  h -1  was designed, as described in Figure 2. Ahead of introducing the syngas stream delivered from the gasification process into the membrane unit, it is initially pressurized to 8 bar and adjusted to a desired operating temperature (Table 2). The membrane area can be modulated to produce the syngas with an optimal H2:CO ratio at a given N2 removal ratio, while the pressure of the retentate stream is maintained constant as the feed pressure. Compressors are necessary for both  the  feed  and  retentate  sides  of  the  membrane  unit,  while  a  vacuum  suction  pump  is required on the permeate side. It should be noted that the present study does not incorporate the heat integration network between the condenser and heat exchanger - this aspect might be explored in future work.
texts.text: Concerning  the  small-scale  scenario  of  syngas  production  from  the  gasification  process,  a single-stage carbon membrane system with a syngas processing capacity of ≤1000 Nm 3  h -1  was designed, as described in Figure 2. Ahead of introducing the syngas stream delivered from the gasification process into the membrane unit, it is initially pressurized to 8 bar and adjusted to a desired operating temperature (Table 2). The membrane area can be modulated to produce the syngas with an optimal H2:CO ratio at a given N2 removal ratio, while the pressure of the retentate stream is maintained constant as the feed pressure. Compressors are necessary for both  the  feed  and  retentate  sides  of  the  membrane  unit,  while  a  vacuum  suction  pump  is required on the permeate side. It should be noted that the present study does not incorporate the heat integration network between the condenser and heat exchanger - this aspect might be explored in future work.

texts.orig: On  the  basis  of  the  separation  performance  of  the  fabricated  carbon  membrane  and  the aforementioned process description, the simulation basis described in Table 2 was employed to  assess the technical feasibility of the carbon membrane system in terms of adjusting the syngas  ratio  and  removing  N2  from  the  gasification  process.  A  typical  syngas  composition consisting of CO2, H2, CO, and N2 in a molar ratio of 15:15:20:50 was adopted while the presence of CH4 in negligible quantities was ignored to streamline the process simulation. The process described  in  Figure  4  was  simulated  using  the  UNISIM ®   simulation  software,  which  is integrated with a tailored membrane model configured with a counter-current flow pattern [34]. Given that removing a significant portion of N2 in the syngas stream is proposed in this study, an N2 removal ratio of 90 % was assumed in the simulation process and the obtained product exhibits potential as a valuable syngas source for downstream methanol and diesel fuel production [35]. Moreover, the pressure drops associated with coolers and heat exchangers were not taken into consideration, and an adiabatic efficiency of 75 % was applied to estimate the energy demand of compressors and vacuum pumps. A comprehensive analysis was carried out on the process parameters including operating temperature and vacuum pressure, as well as a sensitivity analysis of N2 removal ratio, based on the experimental data acquired from the quaternary gas permeation test.

To this end, 203 single gas permeances of H2, CO2, CO, and CH4 were initially measured at different temperatures 204 to serve as references for quantifying their behaviour in mixed gas conditions. The permeance 205 of all single gases increased with temperature, with H2 being the most permeable gas, followed 206 by CO2, CO, and CH4 (Table 3). This agrees with what has already been observed in the literature 207 [ 36 ,  37 ,  38 ],  where  permeability  is  inversely  proportional  to  the  critical  volume  of  the 208 molecules, confirming that diffusion is the dominant transport mechanism (Figure 3- left side). 209 We evaluated the separation properties of the membranes in the presence of three different 210 binary  H2  mixtures  (H2  mixed  with  CO2,  CO,  and  CH4)  at  various  compositions  and  three 211 quaternary mixtures also containing H2, but with a composition closer to real syngas streams, 212 as detailed in 213 Table 1 . 214

texts.orig: diffusion into pores of size comparable with molecule size, the reduction of H2 permeance with increasing CO2 feed molar fraction can be attributed to the hindering effect of CO2 molecules preferentially  adsorbed  on  the  sorption  sites  of  the  membrane  pores  [31].  This  creates  a 'reduction' in the volume available for the diffusion of molecules, resulting in a decrease in H2 permeance. With the increasing of H2 feed molar fraction less CO2 is present in the feed and thus  it  easily  adsorbed  inside  the  membrane  matrix,  with  a  consequent  increase  in  CO2 diffusivity owing to the reverse of coverage [41, 42] which is dominant over the lower sorption, resulting in an increase in CO2 permeance. Similar behaviour was observed for the other binary mixtures of H2 with CO or CH4. In both cases, H2 permeance decreased with respect to single gas conditions as the molar fraction of the other component in the mixture increased, while CO and CH4 permeance increased as the H2 concentration increased. Overall, when compared to the H2:CO2 mixture, the reduction effect on H2 permeance was less significant.

texts.orig: Based on the results obtained, the hollow fibre carbon-based membranes presented in this study  exhibit  preferential  selectivity  towards  H2  and  CO2,  with  selectivity  values  that  vary depending  on  the  operating  conditions  and  feed  composition.  As  such,  they  are  promising candidates for use in syngas upgrading, such as in an integrated biomass-to-biofuel (methanol) process  for  hydrogen  enrichment  or  carbon  capture  and  conversion.  When  examining  the permeate compositions obtained from upgrading a typical syngas mixture (Table 1) produced in  a  biomass  gasification  reactor  [ 43 ],  it  is  evident  that  the  carbon-based  membranes significantly increase the concentration of H2 and CO2, ranging from 98 to 94 % at 50 and 80 °C respectively, while substantially reducing the fraction of N2 and CO, which ranges between 1 to 3.3  %  (Figure  9-left).  The  increase  in  CO  and  N2  concentration  in  the  permeate  with temperature  is  owing  to  the  increased  permeance  of  these  two  gases  and  the  concurrent decrease in CO2 permeance, which is ascribable to the diminished effect of solubility at higher temperatures (Figure 9-right).

texts.orig: The influence of operating temperature on key parameters (e.g., membrane area and power demand)  of  a  single  stage  membrane  unit  used  for  treating  1000  Nm 3   h- 1   of  syngas  was examined under the specific conditions of a 90 % N2 removal ratio and a vacuum pressure of 20 kPa at the membrane permeate side. As depicted in Figure 10 - right side, a considerably larger membrane area of 60539 m 2  at 50 ℃ is necessary in order to offset the reduction in N2 permeance at lower temperatures, in contrast to the comparatively smaller membrane area of 11999 m 2  required at  80 ℃ .  Although  the  permeances  for all gas compositions  at elevated temperatures are improved, there is a slight decrease in CO/N2 selectivity, which brings about a higher CO loss at a given N2 removal ratio. Consequently, the gas flow rate on the permeate site declines, leading to a marginal reduction in power consumption from 148.7 to 148.2 kW. Figure 10 - left side exhibits the impact of vacuum pressure on the required membrane area and power consumption, considering the best membrane performance at 80 ℃ and a specified N2  removal  ratio  of  90  %.  Specifically,  the  required  membrane  area  experiences  a  slight increase, ranging from 11877 to 12397 m 2 , as the vacuum pressure varies from 10 to 50 kPa. Conversely,  the  power  demand  demonstrates  a  general  downward  trend,  decreasing  from 169.3  to  125.2  kW  in  response  to  the  progressive  rise  in  vacuum  pressure.  A  greater transmembrane pressure provides a boosted driving force for gas permeation, contributing to a raised permeate flux along with a lower membrane area. Besides, the increasing gas flux leads to  a  notable  augmentation  in  the  power  consumption  of  both  the  vacuum  pump  and  the compressors. Overall, the comprehensive examination of operating temperature and vacuum pressure provides valuable insights into the interplay of key parameters within the membranebased  system,  contributing  to  optimizing  system  design,  enhancing  energy  efficiency,  and ultimately advancing the feasibility of this technology for further practical application.

texts.orig: The investigation places significant emphasis on the N2 removal ratio owing to the potential impact of N2 impurities on conversion efficiency for downstream methanol production [35]. N2, a  prevalent  impurity,  can  reduce  partial  pressure  and  hinder  the  conversion  process, necessitating a sensitivity analysis. Additionally, the substantial volume occupied by N2 within the reactor incurs higher capital investment costs. As such, a comprehensive sensitivity analysis of the N2 removal ratio (50  - 98 %) was performed to further evaluate the technical feasibility of the membrane system to produce an enriched gas mixture with a desired H2/CO ratio under the conditions of the optimal membrane performance at 80 ℃ and a vacuum pressure of 20 kPa. As depicted in Figure 11 -left side, it can be observed that both the required membrane area and power demand reveal a strong dependence on the N2 removal ratio. An elevated N2 removal  ratio  contributes  to  a  lower  flow  rate  of  the  permeate  stream,  thereby  causing  a considerable  decrease  in  both  the  required  membrane  area  (from  51740  to  2801  m 2 )  and power consumption (from 180.6 to 129.8 kW). Furthermore, it is worth noting that both the H2 and CO recovery experience a substantial drop when the desired N2 removal ratio exceeds 90 %, triggering a drastic increase of the H2:CO ratio in the produced syngas, as highlighted in Figure 11 -right side. To mitigate excessive CO loss and capitalize on the benefits of CO presence in syngas for downstream applications, it is recommended to target an N2 removal ratio range

texts.label: text
texts.orig: of 80 % to 90 %. This range holds the potential for fine-adjusting the syngas ratio to achieve an optimal H2:CO ratio of approximately 2-3. Concerning the inadequate membrane performance in separating CO and N2, future efforts to advance CO-selective membrane materials, enhancing CO/N2 selectivity, will be pivotal in reducing the required membrane area while maintaining a high N2 removal ratio. Additionally, exploring optimized multi-stage membrane systems could further diminish both membrane area requirements and power demands. Last but not least, a further economic evaluation, considering a notable improvement in membrane performance, can  be  also  undertaken  to  assess  the  capital  and  operational  expenditures  associated  with implementing the membrane system for syngas ratio adjustment and N2 removal ratio in a practical scenario.

texts.orig: In this study, we explored the use of carbon hollow fibre membranes, prepared from cellulose precursors,  for  the  separation  of  H2  from  binary  and  quaternary  mixtures.  Our  systematic analysis of the separation properties of these carbon hollow fibre membranes in H2-containing mixtures confirmed that both the permeance and selectivity of mixed gases depend on the type, composition, and temperature of the mixture being treated. Overall, the presence of H2 in the mixture enhances the permeance of other gases owing to a promotional effect on diffusivity,

Our  systematic analysis of the separation properties of these carbon hollow fibre membranes in H2-containing mixtures confirmed that both the permeance and selectivity of mixed gases depend on the type, composition, and temperature of the mixture being treated. Overall, the presence of H2 in the mixture enhances the permeance of other gases owing to a promotional effect on diffusivity,

texts.orig: This effect is more pronounced at higher molar fractions of H2 in the mixture and at higher temperatures.  The  presence  of  CO2,  CH4,  or  CO  tends  to  reduce  H2  permeance  owing  to  a hindering effect caused by gas sorption in the carbon membrane, which is more significant at lower  H2  concentrations  in  the  feed  and  at  lower  temperatures.  Our  experiments  with quaternary mixtures confirmed that the developed carbon membranes exhibit good selectivity for (H2+CO2) in mixtures with other gases such as N2, CH4, and CO, which are commonly present in syngas produced from typical biomass gasification processes.

The  presence  of  CO2,  CH4,  or  CO  tends  to  reduce  H2  permeance  owing  to  a hindering effect caused by gas sorption in the carbon membrane, which is more significant at lower  H2  concentrations  in  the  feed  and  at  lower  temperatures.  Our  experiments  with quaternary mixtures confirmed that the developed carbon membranes exhibit good selectivity for (H2+CO2) in mixtures with other gases such as N2, CH4, and CO, which are commonly present in syngas produced from typical biomass gasification processes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Process simulations were, thus, executed to analyse the technological feasibility of single-stage carbon membrane systems for N2 removal and syngas ratio adjustment from a typical syngas stream produced by biomass gasification. An appreciable increase in the required membrane area and a reduction in power consumption were observed at a desired higher N2 removal ratio, however,  the  enriched  syngas  product  recovery  experiences  a  drastic  decline  when  the  N2 removal ratio exceeds 90 %, indicating that the escalated costs associated with pursuing an excessively high N2 removal ratio may not adequately offset the benefits of space-saving for the downstream  reactor  and  the  overall syngas conversion  efficiency. Consequently, cost minimization should be carried out in future work to identify the optimal N2 removal ratio. Considering the substantial membrane area requirement indicated in this simulation study, it is anticipated that a notable enhancement in H2 and CO permeance could lead to a noteworthy reduction  in  the  required  membrane  area.  Additionally,  it  is  essential  to  emphasize  the significant CO loss for the designed membrane system, which also underscores the urgency for future endeavours dedicated to advancing the development of membranes with elevated H2 and CO permeances as well as excellent CO/N2 selectivity to further improve the competitiveness of carbon membranes for this application.
